text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 14 July 2018

Dr James Patel
Riverside Health Centre

12 Meadow Lane

Bromley BR1 2AB
Dear Dr James Patel
Michael O’Connor, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: M839204 NHS No. 7483926150
Flat 3, 29 Willow Street, London EC2A 4BH 

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset, new diagnosis in 2018; high caffeine intake acknowledged and discussed.

Neuroimaging/EEG: MRI brain requested (pending). Routine EEG arranged; no prior studies available.

Medication history: No prior regular anti-seizure medications. No known drug allergies. Not yet trialled: lamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lacosamide, topiramate, zonisamide.

Relevant background: Non-smoker. Reports high daily caffeine consumption (coffee and energy drinks), particularly late evening. Sleeps irregular hours due to work pattern.

I reviewed Michael O’Connor in clinic today. He attended alone. He reports two events concerning for focal seizures. His first seizure occurred in January 2018 in Ireland, at night while asleep. He woke with rhythmic twitching of the right arm and a sense of déjà vu. The second event was in June 2018 in Scotland, also during sleep, lasting five minutes with a similar pattern of symptoms. There was no tongue bite or urinary incontinence reported. Post-event, he described brief confusion and fatigue. No clear precipitant was identified for either event, though he acknowledges very high caffeine intake and poor sleep around those times.

Between events he describes occasional brief, stereotyped episodes of rising epigastric sensation and déjà vu lasting seconds without loss of awareness, occurring roughly once every few weeks, which may represent focal aware seizures. There is no history of head injury, CNS infection, or family history of epilepsy. Cardiovascular and systemic review otherwise unremarkable.

Examination: Neurological examination today was normal. Cardiovascular and general examination were within normal limits.

Counselling today focused on the likely diagnosis of focal epilepsy with probable temporal lobe onset, the role of sleep deprivation and high caffeine intake as potential facilitators of seizures, and safety advice including driving regulations. He does not currently drive. We discussed the importance of sleep hygiene and reducing caffeine, especially in the afternoon and evening.

Plan:
- Investigations: Arrange MRI brain with epilepsy protocol. Routine EEG (sleep-deprived if feasible) to characterise interictal activity.
- Treatment: Discussed initiating lamotrigine as first-line therapy for focal epilepsy; we agreed to defer initiation until EEG/MRI are completed in the next few weeks unless further events occur. If a further event happens, we will start lamotrigine promptly via nurse-led titration pathway.
- Lifestyle: Strong advice to reduce caffeine intake (aim ≤1–2 cups of coffee equivalent per day and avoid energy drinks), maintain regular sleep schedule, and avoid alcohol excess.
- Safety: Standard seizure precautions discussed, including bathing/showering safety and avoiding heights or operating machinery until stable.
- Follow-up: Neurology follow-up in 8–10 weeks or sooner if further events. Telephone contact provided for interim concerns.

Thank you for your ongoing care and for assisting with investigations and lifestyle reinforcement. Please let me know if there are any issues or if further GP observations arise.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: His first seizure occurred in January 2018 in Ireland, at night while asleep. The second event was in June 2018 in Scotland, also during sleep, lasting five minutes with a similar pattern of symptoms.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: 1) Identify explicit seizure events: The letter states there were two clear seizures. 2) Extract timing: First seizure in January 2018; second in June 2018. 3) Calculate observation window between these events: January (month 1) to June (month 6) gives an elapsed interval of approximately 5 months (6 - 1 = 5). 4) Determine frequency: 2 seizures over ~5 months → 2 per 5 month. 5) Consider other episodes: The letter mentions brief episodes "occurring roughly once every few weeks" that "may represent" focal aware seizures; due to the conditional phrasing and lack of a precise numeric rate, these are not used for the final explicit frequency. 6) Not seizure-free: Last event was June 2018 and clinic date is 14 July 2018, which is <6 months, so not seizure free.
seizure_frequency_number: His first seizure occurred in January 2018 in Ireland, at night while asleep. The second event was in June 2018 in Scotland, also during sleep, lasting five minutes with a similar pattern of symptoms.
